text: Absolute body segment masses (total, lean, and fat) of legs, arms, and trunk show no significant correlation with 5000-m race performance in well-trained endurance runners.
Higher relative leg mass (normalized to body mass) is associated with slower 5000-m race times, with medium positive correlations for both total and lean leg mass.
Higher relative trunk mass (normalized to body mass) is associated with faster 5000-m race times, with large negative correlations for both total and lean trunk mass.
Relative arm segment mass (total, lean, and fat) shows no significant correlation with 5000-m race performance.
A body mass distribution favoring smaller relative leg mass and greater relative trunk mass may be advantageous for endurance running performance.
Lighter legs reduce the metabolic cost of swinging the limbs during running, making relative leg mass more important than absolute leg mass for performance.
